text.content:
The Konungs-ferð

"By the Danelaw of High King Harald, bearer of the Cold Iron Crown, a Konungs-ferð is proclaimed. Let each settlement of loyal standing send forth one champion to Hrafnborg, there to serve the realm's need in this time of shadow and frost."

The words are old. The desperation is new.

What You Know

The Fimbulwinter is in its third year and showing no signs of ending. Across Svellheim, the winters grow longer, the harvests smaller, and the dead restless.

The Konungs-ferð—the King's Journey Duty—is an ancient law, invoked only in the direst need. Its invocation now tells every jarl in Svellheim that High King Harald is out of options.

Each hero was chosen by their community to answer the call. You travel to Hrafnborg, the last great city still standing.

What Every Hero Knows

  • The Fimbulwinter is three years old and getting worse.
  • The dead are rising — not everywhere, but more often, and closer to settled towns.
  • Hrafnborg is the largest settlement still standing. The King is there. Rations are there. Law is there.
  • Refusing a Konungs-ferð stains a family's name for a generation. You didn't have a real choice.

How You Were Chosen

  • How did the Konungs-ferð reach your town — a rider, a skald, a lantern-signal, a raven-sealed letter?
  • How did your community "volunteer" you — a Thing vote, a hearth-oath, a Quiet Warden's suggestion?
  • What did you pack — one personal item that matters to you?

The Road Ahead

You leave behind the only warmth you have ever known. The roads are bad — worse than last year. The mile-markers are buried. The lanterns are dark. The direction is clear: south and west, toward the coast, toward Hrafnborg.

You are not the only one on the road.

The Road to Hrafnborg

The Lantern Road earned its name from brass lamp-posts that once lined it at every quarter-mile. Most are dark now. Some have been torn down for metal. You are strangers walking the same direction for the same grim reason. No one says much.

The Journey

Two or three days along failing roads. Punishing weather: wind off the sea, sudden squalls, frost that creaks under your boots at night. By the end, you are not yet companions — but you are no longer strangers.

The Graveyard

The ruin looms against the grey sky — stone ribs of a long-dead faith. The graveyard sprawls around it: tilted stones, frozen earth, and something wrong in the air. The cold here doesn't just chill your skin. It pulls. Then you hear it: the scrape of bone on frozen ground.

The dead rise. This is the first proof that the old stories are real. They accuse. They remember. They don't stop moving until you make them.

Aftermath

The last draugr crumbles to frost and ash. For a moment, the only sound is your breathing and the wind through the ruin's ribs. Then, from the south, you hear bells. Hrafnborg. You've arrived.

You found a coin on the Barrow-Warden's body — old, worn, stamped with a seven-pointed flame. Someone's token. You don't know whose yet.

The Quiet Summons

You expected processing. Perhaps rest. Instead, a warm room and guards who would not answer questions. The waiting felt wrong. Then the door opened, and there was no herald.

What Harald Told You

High King Harald, older than his reputation, tired, ink-stained, no sword, no guards. He led you through empty corridors to a private room where a grey hound watched from the fire. Then he told you the truth:

  • The Ever-Embers are dying — not ordinary fires, but anchors holding the boundary between the living and the dead.
  • Three weeks ago, the Hrafnborg Ember warned the Keepers it was failing.
  • The night before the King could act, all seven Ember-Keepers were murdered. In their beds. No witnesses.
  • The next morning, the dead attacked the city walls. They have not stopped.
  • Every defensive plan is anticipated. The attacks circumvent every trap. Harald believes there is a leak inside his own court. He doesn't know who.

Your Mission

Find Kaelen. She was the youngest Ember-Keeper ever chosen — thirteen years old when the flame chose her. She served faithfully for a year and then vanished. No message. No reason. Harald believes she is still alive and still carries the ember-mark needed to speak to the flame.

Find her. Bring her back. Svellheim's warmth depends on it.

The First Step: Find Lew Tosferd

A figure named Gragnir stepped from the shadows — tall, lean, grey furs, fingers black with soot. He said the King didn't know where Kaelen was. He did.

There is a man named Lew Tosferd who can find things that are lost. Lew is in Stairwatch, a waystation town beyond Midgard's Shield. ~200 miles away. Ten days through cold and bandit country. Find Lew. Lew will find Kaelen. Lew will name his price when you arrive. He always does.

What Harald Gave You

  • Forged travel papers — presenting you as a merchants' retinue. Don't let anyone look too closely at the seal.
  • Three days' cold-weather rations and two lantern-oil flasks.
  • A sealed cipher packet bearing the King's private mark. Use only in dire need. His last words: "Trust no one in my name. If someone quotes my orders, assume they're lying or compromised."

The Coastal Road

You leave Hrafnborg before dawn, slipping through the Chainwardens' outer checkpoints with forged merchants' papers and the taste of salt pork still on your tongues. The Lantern Road stretches north into a world of grey ice and stunted pines.

The King's instructions are clear: reach Stairwatch. Find Lew Tosferd. Trust no one.

The cold doesn't care about instructions.

What You Notice on the Road

As you travel, the world tells you what you already suspected:

  • The Ever-Ember's warmth faded fast once you left Hrafnborg's radius. The cold deepens with every mile.
  • Roadside shrines to Yggdrasil appear every few miles — small cairns with hollow centres where flame-offerings should sit. Every single one is extinguished. Some have been for weeks.
  • No wildlife. No birds, no hares, no foxes. The only tracks are old. Something has driven everything south or underground.
  • The people you pass on the road don't make eye contact. They walk fast, heads down, clutching whatever they can carry. No one stops to talk.
  • At one point, a farmstead. No smoke from the chimney. The door is open. Snow has drifted inside. Food still on the table — frozen solid. They left in a hurry.

The Frostfen Marshes

Where the Lantern Road crosses the old fenlands, the terrain turns treacherous. The marshes haven't frozen solid — the ice is thin in patches, the reeds hide pockets of black water, and the footcauseway is half-collapsed.

The cold here is different. Wetter. Heavier. Like the land itself is exhaling.

The causeway is the only way through. You cross in the dark or you cross in the cold and hope the ice holds.

The Refugee Road

Beyond the Frostfen, the Lantern Road meets the Herdweg — the old drovers' highway. The junction is choked with refugees fleeing south toward Hrafnborg. Families, merchants, whole villages on the move.

You are heading north. Questions will be asked about why young, healthy travelers are heading toward the danger when everyone else flees from it.

The Hollow Waystation

The road widens at a stone-built waystation — one of the old Embertender rest-houses. The roof is intact. The walls are solid. Smoke rises from the chimney.

Someone is inside.

Bryn Ketilsdottir — a former Embertender who abandoned her post when her settlement's Ever-Ember went out three weeks ago. She has been living here alone, burning furniture for warmth, rationing dried fish.

She is not hostile. She is ashamed. She believes she failed her duty. She didn't. She was in the room when her flame went out, and what she says happened should interest you:

"I heard a sound. Not from outside — from below. Like something exhaling. The flame bent toward the floor and went out. The stone was warm for an hour afterward, as if the fire had sunk into the earth."

She also heard from refugees: three more Ever-Embers have failed since hers. "It's not the cold killing them," she says. "It's something underneath. The ground is sick."

The Mountain Road

The road narrows between two bluffs of dark stone. A wall of stacked timber and iron bars blocks the path -- not quite a military fortification, but close enough. A gate. A guardhouse. A flag bearing a broken chain, re-forged.

Two wardens stand at the gate, wrapped in furs over mail. One holds a ledger. The other holds a spear.

"Papers," says the one with the ledger. It is not a request.

The Greypass Toll

Warden-Sergeant Eirik Greyhand has been running the Greypass checkpoint for eleven months without a resupply. He is not corrupt. He is exhausted -- and exhausted men with authority are their own kind of obstacle.

He wants order. He wants to go home. He has not heard from his family in Hrafnborg in a month.

You can give him news that matters, or supplies that stretch longer than promises. Or you can try to talk past him. Either way, the road past this gate is the only one that goes through to the mountains.

The Caravan at the Crossroads

A caravan has made camp in the lee of a collapsed waystation wall -- five covered wagons, a mule string, and a fire that burns brighter than it has any right to on a night like this.

A woman stands at the fire's edge as you approach. She is carrying something heavy -- not in her arms, but in the way she stands, like a person who has been carrying the same thing for a very long time.

"Traders don't usually come this way," she says. Not an accusation. An observation. "The road gets worse up ahead. Sit. There's stew."

Soldis Glodfari, a caravan leader who has been running this route for six years, knows every rockfall, every patrol schedule, and every warm hollow on the mountain road. Her caravan feeds you, patches your gear, and asks for nothing but honesty in return.

She carries a sealed clay pot she will not discuss. When asked about it: "That's for later."

If she notices something unusual about one of you -- and she will, eventually -- she says only: "You carry something. I just noticed the shadow it casts."

The Frozen Messenger

A figure sits against a roadside boulder, hunched in a cloak. Snow has built up against one shoulder like a small drift. Their skin is blue-grey, their eyes open and glazed with frost.

A leather satchel hangs from one frozen hand.

Dead four days, by the look of it. A Chainwarden runner, by the badge.

The satchel holds three things: two days of frozen rations, a Chainwarden map of the Titan's Stair pass with hazard marks annotated in red ink -- and a wax-sealed letter addressed to The Watch-Commander, Stairwatch.

The seal is the King's cipher. But it is not Harald's personal seal. Someone else in the court sent this.

You have not opened it yet.

Dead on the Mountain Road

Night. The campfire is the only light for miles. The sky is overcast -- no moon, no stars.

Then, from the darkness beyond the fire's edge: the slow, deliberate crunch of something heavy moving through frozen snow. Not one set of footsteps. Two.

And smaller sounds, flicker-quick, circling the light at the edge of vision -- like sparks that don't go out.

The Frost-Draugr that emerge from the dark are not new dead. When you get close enough to see the armour fragments and corroded brooches, they look ancient -- centuries old. Whatever is raising the dead is not just waking recent bodies. It's reaching deep.

Grave-Wisps swirl around them, trailing cold. They want to pull you toward the draugr. The wisps see your campfire as competition.

The last draugr crumbles to hoarfrost that melts in the fire's heat. The wisps scatter like sparks and blink out.

Silence. The fire crackles. The wind moans through the broken walls.

Nobody sleeps well after that.

The Cairn Field

Near the summit of Titan's Stair, the path widens into a flat plateau. Cairns stand here -- dozens of them, rough-stacked stone pillars ranging from knee-height to taller than a man. Each one marked with runes.

Travel-cairns. Monuments left by generations of travellers who survived this crossing. The tradition: add a stone, speak a name, and the crossing grants safe passage.

Some are recent. One is barely a day old -- a small stack with a child's mitten tucked between the stones.

The newest cairn has no runes. Just a word scratched into the topstone with a knife:

HELP.

The wind drops if you add a stone. For exactly one minute, you can feel the sun's warmth through the clouds -- faint, but real.

Stairwatch

Stairwatch hunches at the foot of Titan's Stair like a dog that's been kicked too many times. It's a waystation town -- built for travellers who never stay long, and it shows. The buildings lean. The streets are mud frozen into ridges. Smoke rises from every chimney, but the fires burn low.

The inn-sign reads The Broken Stair. Below it, smaller: Rooms. Ale. No questions.

The town's Ever-Ember burns in a stone ring at the central crossroads. It's the smallest you've seen -- barely a campfire.

But it burns. It burns.

Two hundred miles. Ten days of cold road, failing bridges, checkpoints, and the dark.

You are here. Somewhere in this town is the person who can find Kaelen.

Find Lew Tosferd.

Finding Lew

Stairwatch is a town that forgot to die. The buildings lean together over narrow streets, their turf roofs frosted white, their chimneys leaking smoke that smells of wet wood and roasted barley. At the central crossroads, the Ever-Ember burns in a stone ring -- small, barely a campfire's worth, but warm. Genuinely warm.

A teenager stands near it, arms wrapped around herself, watching the flame.

The Broken Stair

The inn is exactly what the sign promises: low ceilings, three rooms upstairs, a common hall with a fire that's always burning, and a dwarf named Hild who asks no questions and charges fair prices.

When you ask about "the Finder": "The one-eyed man? Rented a room, owes me for three nights. Try the chandler's -- the shop at the top of the hill with the shutterless window. He whittles on the bench outside when the light's good."

The Chandler's

A man sits on a bench outside a shop that smells of tallow and beeswax. He is whittling something from a piece of bone -- small, careful strokes, the knife moving with the patience of someone who has nowhere else to be.

One eye is dark, watchful, human. The other catches the grey light wrong. It gleams faintly amber -- not glass, not stone. Something else.

He doesn't look up as you approach.

"Three strangers with road-dust on their boots and a look like someone's given them a job they didn't ask for." The knife keeps moving. "Let me guess -- someone sent you. Someone important. And they told you I could find something."

He looks up. "Name's Lew. Sit down. You're making the chandler nervous."

Llewyellen "Lew" Tosferd. The Finder. He is warm, sharp, and entirely without sentiment about money. He listens to your pitch before naming his price. He asks questions -- about you, your homes, your skills.

He never lies. He tells you exactly as much as he's decided to tell you. No more.

The Deal

Lew is willing to help -- for a price. He wants two things:

  1. A favour, to be named later. Not a threat. An IOU. "One day, I'll need something found. Or someone kept unfound. And you'll help. Fair?"

  2. Help with a local problem first. Lew can't walk out of Stairwatch without handling his obligations. He needs your help before he'll leave.

The Eye

After you've settled his local problem, Lew finds you at the Broken Stair.

He taps the amber eye -- the one that catches the light wrong.

"Lost the real one years ago. Bad job, bad luck, bad timing. Gragnir pulled me out. Stitched me up. And when I was healing, he gave me this."

He taps it again. The amber gleams.

"Took me months to realise what it could do. I was looking for a lost shipment of pelts and the eye just... pulled. Like a compass needle. Pointed me right to the cache."

He leans forward. "It finds lost things. People. Objects. Places. If something was known and became unknown -- the eye pulls toward it."

A pause.

"Let me try."

Lew closes his good eye. For a moment, nothing happens. Then the amber eye shifts -- not the lid, not the socket, but the light inside the gem. It brightens, dims, brightens again, like a heartbeat.

His hand comes up slowly and points southeast. Through the wall of the inn. Through the mountain. Toward the vast grey emptiness of the Interior Belt.

"She's there," he says. "Past the Shield. Past the dead farms. Deep in the Ashen Woods -- what's left of them." His voice is quiet. "She's alive. And she doesn't want to be found."

He opens his eye. The amber fades.

"That's the Great Green, friends. Or what used to be it. Three weeks' travel if we're lucky. More if we're not." He pauses. "And we won't be."

Southeast. Deep in the Ashen Woods. Alive.

Kaelen doesn't want to be found.

Before You Leave

The morning of departure. Hild packs extra rations from the inn's stores -- more than she should, given how thin the shelves are. She doesn't explain. When Lew tries to thank her, she cuts him off: "Bring that one-eyed bastard back alive. He owes me."

At the Ever-Ember, the teenager -- Solvi, the Ember-Keeper -- stops you. She presses a small iron box into your hands. Inside: a coal-ember fragment, still warm in a way that doesn't make sense for this cold.

"Take this," she says. "It's not much. But..." She searches for words. "Don't let the fire die. Please."

At the gate, a crow perches on the post. Silver eyes. It tilts its head as you pass, watches you take the road southeast -- then lifts away, heading south.

The road ahead leads through failing farmland, abandoned villages, and into the skeletal remains of the Great Greenweald -- now called the Ashen Woods.

A forest that burned. A woman who is hiding. A compass made of amber and an eye that was paid for with blood.

Find her. Bring her home.

The Ashen Woods

The first sign is the smell: charcoal and wet ash, as if the fire that burned here never quite stopped smouldering. Then the trees begin.

They aren't trees anymore. They're pillars of charcoal -- sixty, seventy feet tall, stripped of bark and branch, standing in rows that go on forever. The Great Greenweald was a forest. Now it's a graveyard of wood.

Nothing moves. Nothing calls. The only sound is the crunch of char under your boots and the occasional crack of a dead trunk giving way to its own weight.

The sky is grey. The ground is grey. The trees are black.

And deep in the distance, Lew's eye pulls you on.

The Journey

~160 miles through the Interior Belt and the Ashen Woods. Nine days Lew estimates. The silence here is aggressive -- it pushes back against conversation, against laughter, against the sound of your own breathing.

By the end of day one, you are grey-black from head to foot with charcoal dust. It gets in your clothes, your lungs, your eyes. At night it settles on everything.

"She's still there," Lew says, every morning and every evening. "Still alive. Still hiding."

The Cathedral

The trees change -- not in colour, still black, still dead -- but in arrangement. The trunks here grow closer together, and their upper branches interlock overhead, forming a natural vault. Ash motes drift downward like black snow.

It looks like a cathedral. A vast nave of dead wood, stretching southeast into darkness.

And on a flat stone slab in the clearing at the cathedral's centre: a bowl of still-warm porridge, a clay cup of water, and a sprig of green holly.

Someone is tending this place. Someone alive and nearby.

If you leave food or a tool in return, a sound you haven't heard in these woods: a human throat clearing. A woman emerges from behind a pillar, terribly thin, skin stained grey-black with years of charcoal dust.

Solveig has lived in the Ashen Woods for three years. She refused to leave when the forest burned. She burns charcoal for fuel, navigates by the root-patterns beneath the ash, and knows every blighted beast's territory.

"You're heading for the green place," she says. It is not a question.

She can point you toward a shortcut and warn you about what lies ahead: "There are things that chew the green roots. Big as dogs. Bigger. They come from the deep ash, and they're getting bolder."

The Green Thread

Lew stops. He kneels and brushes ash away from the ground. Beneath the char -- green. A single root, thick as a man's arm, running through the dead soil. Alive. Pale green bark, sap beading at the surface, warm to the touch.

He follows it with his fingers. It runs southeast -- the same direction the eye has been pulling.

"That's not a tree root," he says quietly. "That's a taproot. Something at the centre of these woods is still alive. Feeding the whole forest from below, trying to bring it back."

He looks up. "That's where she is."

The Singing Stone

The path opens into a clearing. At its centre stands a single upright boulder of dark granite -- taller than a man, roughly cylindrical.

It's humming. Not audibly -- you feel it in your teeth, your fingertips, the soles of your boots. Ash around the stone has been pushed aside, revealing bare earth and roots. Dozens of roots, all converging on the stone like spokes on a wheel.

Lew presses his palm against the stone. His eye flares bright amber.

"The Tree," he whispers. "This stone is sitting on one of the Tree's roots. A deep one. I can feel it all the way down. Miles down."

If you touch the stone: warmth -- real warmth, like standing beside a great fire. For one moment, the Fimbulwinter cold lifts entirely. Then it returns, and the warmth fades, and the world is grey again.

The Boundary

The Queen's body twitches once, twice, then goes still. The grubs scatter into the ash. The clearing is quiet -- but a different kind of quiet than the dead woods. This silence is waiting.

The living roots pulse brighter. And deep in the southeast, very faint, you hear something you haven't heard since Hrafnborg.

Birdsong.

Lew points. "She's close. Maybe a day's travel. Maybe less."

The charcoal forest gives way to impossible green. Living trees -- real trees, with leaves and bark and birds -- rise from the ash like a miracle. A wall of thorns and vines marks the boundary of the Green Heart.

Kaelen is inside. She knows you're coming. She's been watching through the trees.

The Bone Fields

East of Stairwatch, the mountains give way to broken flatland -- a vast, wind-scoured basin where nothing grows and the wind never stops.

The name is literal. Bones jut from the frozen earth: ribs the size of longships, femurs as tall as pine trees, skulls half-buried with eye sockets wide enough to camp in. These are the bones of giants -- or something older -- fallen in a war so ancient that even the skalds have forgotten which side won.

Between the bones: graves. Thousands of them. Stone-ringed barrows, a day's march in every direction, their capstones cracked and frost-heaved and -- in too many cases -- open.

Lew stands at the edge of the Fields and goes very still.

"Last time I was here," he says quietly, "the ground split open and something came out of the earth. Something old." He touches his amber eye. "That's where this came from."

He takes a breath. "Stay close. Don't touch the bones. And for the love of all that's warm, don't open any barrows."

The Journey

~125 miles. Seven days. Shorter than the Ashen Woods but brutally exposed -- no cover, no shelter. The wind is a physical force: constant, bone-dry, cold enough to freeze exposed skin in minutes. At night, the wind carries sounds that aren't wind: voices, scraps of language, names. The dead in the barrows talk in their sleep.

Lew's Story

By the campfire, sheltered in the lee of a giant's shoulder blade, Lew stares into the flame. The amber eye reflects it strangely -- the light bends toward it, as if the eye is drinking warmth.

"Gragnir brought me here three years ago. A job -- finding something he'd lost. A seed, he said. The size of my fist, wrapped in leather. He'd left it here 'for safekeeping.'" Lew laughs bitterly. "In the Bone Fields."

He taps his bad eye. "We found the seed. We also found something else. The ground cracked open beneath us -- not an earthquake. Something deliberate. Like the earth was opening its mouth. Out of the crack came something big. Old. Made of roots and bone and frozen soil. It looked at Gragnir and spoke."

A long pause.

"Gragnir spoke back. In a language I don't know -- something that made my teeth ache and my eye bleed. The thing listened. Then it went back into the earth, and Gragnir was holding the seed and my eye was gone."

He touches the amber gem. "He put this in the socket himself. Said it was 'fair exchange for what the earth took.' I didn't understand then."

He looks at you. "I still don't. But I can feel the green thread, and I can find what's lost, and my dead eye sees more than the living one ever did."

The Dead at Night

The dead lie down. The singer's voice fades into the wind. The barrows are quiet again -- but the silence feels temporary, like the pause between breaths.

Lew kneels beside one of the draugr. Its armour is old -- generations old -- but the runes embossed on the breastplate are still legible. He traces them with a finger.

"These weren't warriors," he says. "They were farmers. Look -- ploughshare runes, harvest-knots. They died before the giants fell. Before any of this."

He stands. "The Fields go deep. Deeper than bone. Let's not be here when something deeper decides to wake up."

The Frozen Steppe

Beyond the Bone Fields, the terrain flattens into featureless tundra. No landmarks, no features, nothing but grey sky and frozen ground. The silence here isn't like the woods -- it's emptiness. It gets inside your head.

Lew stops more often. His eye burns hotter as he gets closer. Twice he corrects his heading mid-stride, feeling something only he can feel.

"Look at the sky," Lew says.

You look. It takes a moment to see it. The sky over the Steppe is grey -- the same grey it's been for a week. But to the south, near the horizon, the grey is different. Darker. Richer. Not storm-clouds -- something organic. Something that absorbs light rather than blocking it.

"That's the canopy," Lew says. His voice is awed. "The trees. The real trees. The Heart is still growing."

He points. "Two days. Maybe less."

The Boundary

The Queen's body twitches once, twice, then goes still. The grubs scatter. The clearing is quiet -- but a different kind of quiet. This silence is waiting.

The living roots pulse brighter. And deep in the southeast, very faint, you hear something you haven't heard since Hrafnborg.

Birdsong.

Lew points. "She's close. Maybe a day's travel. Maybe less."

The dead terrain gives way to impossible green. Living trees -- real trees, with leaves and bark and birds -- rise like a miracle from the frozen ground. A wall of thorns and vines marks the boundary of the Green Heart.

Kaelen is inside. She knows you're coming. She's been watching through the trees.

The Reluctant Hearth

Entering the Green Heart

The change happens over the course of a mile — and then all at once. The charcoal pillars thin. Ash gives way to soil — real soil, dark and damp. A single green fern uncurls from the ground, then another, then dozens, then hundreds, and suddenly the world is alive.

Trees. Real trees. Birch and ash and oak, winter-bare but breathing — bark intact, roots gripping living earth, branches reaching for a sky that seems somehow less grey here. Snow dusts the ground, but beneath it: moss, roots, the smell of wet earth and growing things.

A wall of thorns — five feet thick, eight feet tall — blocks the path forward. Living bramble, dense as castle walls. Within the thorns, you can hear birdsong. You can feel warmth. Not fire-warmth. Growing-warmth.

Something moves in the canopy above you. A face — bark-skinned, hollow-eyed, beautiful and terrible — watches you from the leaves and says nothing.

The thorn-wall shifts. Not opens — shifts. The brambles pull apart like fingers unclenching, leaving a gap just wide enough for one person at a time.

A voice drifts from deeper in the grove: "Come in. Leave weapons sheathed. The trees are watching."

The Green Heart

The grove is warm. Not Hrafnborg-warm — not the press of bodies and braziers and desperation — but earth-warm. The warmth rises from below, from the roots that pulse with amber light. The air smells of resin and rain.

A clearing opens at the centre: perhaps a hundred feet across, ringed by ancient oaks. Their canopies interlock overhead, forming a living ceiling. Moss carpets the ground. A stream — clear, unfrozen, impossible — runs through the clearing's centre.

Near the stream, a young woman sits cross-legged on a stone. She's wearing practical traveling clothes — patched, repaired, forest-stained. Her hair is dark, cropped short. On her forehead, a scar glows faintly amber in the grove's light: the ember-mark.

She doesn't look up as you enter. She's carving something into a piece of bark with a small knife. Her hands are steady. Her jaw is tight.

"I know why you're here," she says. "The answer is no."

This is Kaelen — the Seventh. Youngest Ember-Keeper ever chosen. She served faithfully for three years — then vanished without explanation. The ember-mark on her forehead still burns, twelve years later.

The Farewell

Kaelen walks the perimeter of the grove one last time. She touches each tree. She speaks to each dryad in a language you don't understand — older than Norrœnt, older than anything you've heard. The words sound like roots growing.

The dryads don't cry. But the leaves around them darken, and the birdsong fades to silence.

At the thorn-wall, Kaelen stops. She presses her palm to the brambles. They part for her — slowly, reluctantly — and on the other side is the grey.

"Don't look back," the lead dryad says. Not to Kaelen. To you. "If she looks back, she won't leave."

Kaelen doesn't look back.

You step through. The thorns close behind you. And the warmth of the Green Heart cuts off like a door slamming shut.

The Long Road Home

~220 miles. ~12 days. You know the road — but the road has changed.

Leaving the Green Heart

The thorn-wall closes behind you. One moment, green — light, birdsong, the smell of living earth. The next: ash. Silence. Grey.

Kaelen walks at the rear of the group. She hasn't spoken since the wall sealed. Lew, for once, doesn't fill the silence. His eye is dim — the amber barely a glow. "She doesn't want to be tracked," he murmurs to you. "The Heart is hiding itself. Even from me."

The dead trees stand like gravestones. And you walk.

Twelve days. Over two hundred miles. The longest road yet.

The Waystation

Inside: bodies.

Not undead. Not risen. Just dead. Seven people — a family, by their clothing. Two adults, three children, two elders. They died huddled together around a fire that went out. Their supplies are scattered but mostly intact.

They didn't die of cold. They died of despair. The elder's hand still holds a knife. There are marks on the floor — tally marks, scratched into stone. Fourteen days' worth.

They waited for help that never came.

The Column

The column stretches back further than you can see. They walk in rough formation — three abreast, shields hanging loose, weapons rusted but functional. Their eyes are the dead grey of bog ice.

Kaelen watches them pass. "They're not just moving," she says. "They're mustering. Someone is calling them."

Lew touches his eye. "Southwest. Always southwest. Same direction as Hrafnborg."

The column takes twenty minutes to pass. None of the dead look at you. They have orders. You are not part of them.

Kaelen's ember-mark flares once, bright enough to cast a shadow. Two or three draugr turn their heads — slowly, mechanically — and Kaelen steps back and presses her palm over the mark, hiding the light.

The draugr turn away. The column moves on.

"Don't do that again," Lew says quietly.

Greyfen Holdfast

A voice calls from behind the barricade.

"That's far enough. State your business or take the long way round."

The Holdfast at Greyfen is led by Thyra Blackhand — a former Chainwarden sergeant who gathered survivors to this fortified position. Thirty people behind improvised walls. Terrified. Armed. Desperate.

She needs passage, news, and hope. She has supplies and intelligence about the mountain pass.

The Summit

At the summit of Titan's Stair, the wind drops. Just for a moment — a gap between gusts, a breath of stillness in the blizzard.

And from the summit, looking south toward the coast, you see it.

Hrafnborg.

The Ever-Ember's light is visible even through the storm — a warm amber glow on the horizon, impossibly bright and impossibly small. Like a candle in a cathedral.

But around it: darkness. Not storm-darkness. Something else. A stain on the land, surrounding the city in every direction.

Kaelen's ember-mark blazes. She gasps and grabs at her forehead.

"It's still lit," she says. "The Ember's still burning." She pauses. "But it's screaming. Something is pressing against it. Something cold and old and patient."

Lew squints into the distance. "What am I looking at? Is that — are those —"

"Yes," Kaelen says. "The dead."

Gragnir's Reveal

The fire pops. A log shifts.

And Gragnir is there.

He's sitting on the other side of the fire, as if he's been there for hours. His soot-stained hands rest on his knees. His grey furs are dusted with ash. He looks tired — not the fatigue of travel, but the exhaustion of someone who has been fighting a war no one else can see.

"You found her," he says, looking at Kaelen. His voice is rough. "Good."

"Your King has a companion," Gragnir says quietly. "A grey hound. Sits at his feet during every council. Sleeps at the foot of his bed. Follows him to the Ever-Ember chamber. Never barks. Never eats. Just watches."

"It is not a dog. It is not a wolf. It is a shape-walker — a spy. And it has been listening to every word spoken in the King's presence since before you arrived in Hrafnborg."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"It is listening right now. Over two hundred miles away. Sitting beside your King in the dark."

"Its master's name is Grafvitnir," Gragnir says. The word falls like a stone. "The Grave-Wolf. An entity older than Svellheim, older than the Ember-Keepers, older than anything that walks above ground."

"Grafvitnir was imprisoned during the First Kindling — when the original Ember-Keepers sealed the root-chamber beneath Yggdrasil's deepest root. It was not killed. It cannot be killed. It was bound — by fire, by oath, by the roots of the World Tree itself."

"Every Ever-Ember that fails is a lock that opens. Every Keeper who dies is a chain that breaks. When the last Ember goes dark — Grafvitnir walks free."

Kaelen's face goes white. "He heard everything. When Harald told us about the Ever-Ember. When he gave us our mission. Every name, every route, every supply count."

Lew is very still. "That's how they always knew where we were. Not a tracker on the road. A listener in the throne room."

The Siege of Hrafnborg

You have been gone for weeks. The siege started the day you left.

The Silent Ring

You smell them before you see them.

Not rot — cold. The same unnatural chill as the frost-ghouls on the road, scaled a thousandfold. You crest a ridge and the valley opens beneath you, and there it is: Hrafnborg, the Ever-Ember's amber light still burning on its roof — and surrounding it in every direction, three bodies deep, standing still as standing stones, the dead.

Thousands of them. Not attacking. Not moving. Waiting.

The siege ring holds the city without violence. The dead stand in formation, patient as ice. Scouts say they've been there for nineteen days — and not one has broken ranks to attack. Not until something provokes them.

Breaking the Line

The southwest approach means the tannery marshes — abandoned stilt-platforms and reeking brine channels. The ground between the dead is tight, every footstep a decision. Kaelen keeps her ember-mark pressed to her palm, the light suppressed. Without it, she looks ordinary. With it, every draugr within twenty feet turns their head.

Move slow. Move quiet. Move like you belong among the dead.

The marsh is not empty — frost-ghouls stalk between the standing dead, faster and hungrier, drawn by warmth. The party fights through two montage rounds and a sharp engagement before reaching the city walls.

Inside the Walls

The city smells of boiled leather and blood. Every public space has been converted: the great hall into a triage ward, the market square into a ration line, the temple into a surgery. People move quickly and quietly, the way people move when they've learned that noise costs something.

Above, on the keep's roof: the Ever-Ember. Still burning — but smaller. Tired. The amber light that once filled the valley struggles to reach the eaves.

Reunion with Harald

Harald is armoured. He wasn't the last time you saw him — kings don't walk their own halls in plate. Now he does. The crown of the Cold Iron Throne sits over a mail coif, and his face beneath it is gaunt, hollowed by nineteen days of not enough sleep and too many decisions. He looks at you for a long moment before he speaks.

"You found her," he says. Not a question. He's looking at Kaelen. His voice carries something that might be relief, if it had anywhere to land.

At his feet, the grey hound lifts its head.

Its eyes are gold. They are absolutely still. They do not blink.

The Ritual

In the Ember Chamber, Kaelen approaches the flame.

She reaches out — and the flame reaches back.

What follows isn't peaceful. Her eyes go white. The ember blazes, then gutters, then blazes again. Her knees buckle and she catches herself on the altar stone. She screams once — not in pain, but in grief, as if she's just learned something she can't unknow.

When she lifts her face, the ember is steady for the first time in weeks. And she is very, very still.

"I know where we need to go," she says. "I know what the tree needs. And I know what we're up against."

She turns to face you.

"It's worse than Harald told us."

The Breach

The attack begins at the southwest wall. Not the patient, standing stillness of the siege — this is coordinated. Three thousand dead moving in formation toward a single point, driving like a wedge.

And at the front of the column: a thing in corroded mail and black iron, crowned in a circlet of dark metal. Eyes of pale green fire. A banner of frozen bone. It walks at the pace of a general surveying a battlefield.

It reaches the breach in the wall-walk and stops. And it speaks.

Its voice is grinding stone on stone, and the words carry above the wind and the battle-din:

"Give us the Keeper. Give us the flame. And the wall stands."

From somewhere behind you, Harald's voice: "Hold the wall."

The Shape-Walker's Move

A runner arrives from the keep interior. Young. Bleeding from a gash across his forehead. His voice cracks. "The King's chambers — something's wrong — we heard screaming, the guards aren't answering—" He grabs the nearest arm. "The hound. Someone said they saw the hound walk upright."

The Wight-Lord is on the wall. Harald is alone in the King's chambers. And the grey hound is no longer a grey hound.

Inside the King's inner chambers: Harald, backed to his war-table, holds a hunting knife and a candlestick and is absolutely, unmistakably furious rather than afraid.

Across from him stands something that used to be a grey hound. It is no longer a grey hound.

The man — or the idea of a man — is tall, grey-pale, features that shift when you look directly at him. His eyes are gold. They are too still. They do not blink. His voice, when he speaks, is pleasant. Educated.

"You made better time than expected," he says. He does not move. He is not afraid. He is calculating.

The raven hits the window ledge, folds — and then it's just a grey shape dropping into the dark over the siege ring.

Something small clatters to the stone floor. A talon — curved, black as obsidian, still warm. It fell from the raven's grasp during the shift, torn free by the violence of the transformation.

Then the raven is gone.

Harald lowers the knife. His hand is shaking, though his face has gone very still.

"I invited that thing into my hall," he says. His voice is very quiet. "I let it sleep at my feet."

He looks at the window for a long time.

"How long has it been listening?"

What the Ember Said

Afterwards, in the Ember Chamber, Kaelen gives her report:

"The Ever-Embers are not hearth-fires. They never were. They're leaves. Yggdrasil's warmth flows upward through the roots, surfaces at the Embers, and spreads outward. Every Ember is a node in a living system — warmth, life, the boundary between the living and the dead. All of it flows from the tree."

"Yggdrasil is dying. Something — Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf — has been gnawing at the deepest root since the First Kindling. The prison is the root. And as the root weakens, the Embers fed by it are guttering."

"The flame showed me the Emberwell. The place where the first Ember was kindled, where the root surfaces. If the tree can be healed — or the prison reforged — it has to happen there."

Harald's Decision

"I cannot lead this mission. Hrafnborg needs a king on the walls. But I can give you what I have: supplies, maps, authority."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"You've proven yourselves. You found Kaelen. You broke the siege line. You held the wall. I trust you with what comes next — not because I have faith, but because I have no other option."

His voice cracks, just once.

"Go to the Emberwell. Find out what the tree needs. Do what must be done. And come back."

The Road North

You leave Hrafnborg at dawn. The guards open the postern gate — the same one you entered through — and you slip out into the grey.

The dead are still there. But they've pulled back from the southwestern marsh. Not far. Just enough. Kaelen's ember-mark glows faintly, and the nearest draugr flinch.

It won't last. But for now, it clears a path.

The road north stretches ahead. The Interior Belt. The White Waste beyond. And somewhere, at the end of the world, the Emberwell.

Behind you, Hrafnborg's Ever-Ember flickers. Ahead, the wind rises.

You walk.
